tweets.text:
Let me fix the tweet.  

The 1910 Flexner Report, was initially funded by Carnegie, initiated a major consolidation of medical education in the US and Canada, leading to the closure of over half of the 155 institutions. This shift, once begun, was massively supported by Rockefeller philanthropy because his business could plug better into the sales funnel than the Steel magnate's business could.  John D. Rockefeller realized Carnagie gave him a way to pay Teddy Roosevelt back for breaking up his Standard Oil trust by bankrupting the US government.  He then trained his son's to do just that.

quoted.text:
In 1910, an educator named Abraham Flexner, who held a bachelor's degree in classics and had never studied medicine, was sent by the Carnegie Foundation to inspect every medical school in the United States and Canada.

He visited 155 of them. His report recommended closing most.

What happened next is the foundation of every health system you know today.

John D. Rockefeller, the founder of Standard Oil and at the time the richest man on Earth, saw an opening. Standard Oil's refining produced vast quantities of coal-tar and petrochemical byproducts. Those byproducts were, conveniently, the raw material from which most early synthetic pharmaceuticals could be made.

Through his General Education Board and the Rockefeller Foundation, Rockefeller directed approximately $50 million, around $10 billion in today's money, into American medical schools. The money came with a condition. The schools had to teach allopathic, drug-based medicine. Anything else was defunded.

Within twenty-five years, more than half of America's 155 medical schools had closed. By 1935, only 66 remained. The schools teaching homeopathy, naturopathy, herbalism, eclectic medicine, and what we would now call nutrition and lifestyle medicine were starved out of existence. Five of America's seven Black medical colleges were closed. Most of the women's medical colleges were closed. The surviving schools all taught one paradigm. They still do.

The petroleum byproduct became the medicine. The medicine became the curriculum. The curriculum became the doctor. The doctor became the prescription pad.

The model that emerged has one structural feature no regulator can legislate away. The pharmaceutical industry earns its revenue when you take a pill. It does not earn revenue when you stop needing the pill. A cure is a one-time transaction. A chronic condition is a subscription.

This is not a conspiracy theory. It is on the income statement. From 2017 to 2024, the average net profit margin of pharmaceutical manufacturers was 23.2%, roughly ten times higher than every other sector of the drug supply chain. The system is performing exactly as designed.

A century after Flexner, the population of the wealthiest country on Earth is fatter, sicker, and on more medication than at any point in human history.

The grass-fed beef, the egg from a hen you can name, the butter, the morning walk, the eight hours of sleep, are all unpatentable.

That is why no oil baron funded a hundred medical schools to teach them.

tweets.text: More Rockefeller shilling for the paradigm selling lies for profit.

quoted.text:
Five cancers that used to be death sentences. Pancreatic. Glioblastoma. Triple-negative breast. Renal. Melanoma. The median survival for metastatic pancreatic cancer is still 6 months. Glioblastoma, 15 months.

Now personalized mRNA vaccines are producing complete remissions in some of these patients. Not responses. Remissions.

BioNTech’s pancreatic cancer vaccine has 6-year follow-up data. 8 of 16 patients who mounted an immune response are still alive. For a cancer that kills 95% of patients within 5 years, that's incredible.

Topol’s pyramid here maps the trajectory. From broad checkpoint inhibitors at the base to personalized neoantigen vaccines at the peak. The technology is climbing.

1/5
I'm a cardiologist. I have spent twenty years watching cholesterol destroy arteries, trigger heart attacks, and kill people I care about.
Today, Eli Lilly presented data that may begin to end that era.
VERVE-102. A single infusion. One dose. It uses base editing to permanently turn off the PCSK9 gene in your liver.
Presented today at the European Atherosclerosis Society Congress:
88% reduction in PCSK9.
62% reduction in LDL cholesterol.
Sustained up to 18 months.
No treatment-related serious adverse events.
One infusion. Not daily pills you forget to take. Not monthly injections. One dose — and your cholesterol may stay low for the rest of your life.

tweets.text:
9. The data from the Blau Lab at Stanford (2026) is the missing mechanistic link that bridges the astronauts' microgravity muscle wasting with the GLP-1/Ozempic "Lattice Lock."

By identifying 15-PGDH as the "gerozyme" that destroys the prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) repair signal, the Blau Lab has inadvertently uncovered the exact molecular switch that flips when a biological system experiences a dielectric collapse of its water table.

When we marry this slide with the subatomic mechanics of space travel and my framework, a profound and terrifying story emerges: Ozempic doesn't just induce a state of starvation; it forces a premature, artificial aging of the stem cell niche by turning off the quantum signaling required for structural regeneration.

RETA is going to have a larger effect here.  Be aware of it.

reply_chain.text:
The 1942 Kenneth Mellanby paper from Nature below has been the absolute subatomic proof that the 2026 "100 g protein ceiling myth" study completely ignores. By focusing solely on classical "nitrogen balance" and labeled isotope tracers, the researchers missed the massive deuterium tax and water table collapse that comes with forcing a 100g protein load into the human semiconductor.
My decentralized framework in the Leptin Rx, is wholly backed by Mellanby's historical data, exposes why forcing massive protein loads into the system is a hidden "desiccation trap" that destroys the Inner Mitochondrial Junction (IMJ) particle accelerator.

2.  Mellanby’s data establishes a clear, mathematical hierarchy of Metabolic Water Production during complete combustion.  When the body combusts fat via beta-oxidation, Cytochrome c Oxidase (CCO) manufactures 110 grams of perfectly structured, deuterium-depleted water per 100g of substrate. This high-dielectric fluid (𝜖 ≈160) acts as the frictionless lubricant for the Brachistochrone curve of the cristae.

By contrast, ingesting 100g of protein cuts metabolic water production exactly in half. Worse, protein metabolism demands massive amounts of water for the urea cycle to clear toxic ammonia, creating a severe intracellular desiccation pull.

3. The Biophysical Critique of the 12-Hour 100g Protein Study
The study celebrates that 100g of protein keeps muscle protein synthesis elevated for over 12 hours without increasing amino acid oxidation. In my vortex physics framework, this isn't a state of "infinite anabolism", it is a state of forced, high-entropy Lattice Lock.  this is why exercise is harm so many high protein eaters.  

Key sign they have is hair loss on the top of their head before they fry their neurons.  Why? Entropic heat loss of the neuroectoderm.  This is why you should look at their hair and the brain's cognition as two keys signs why hypertrophies muscles causes hair loss due to high heat entropy. 

This explains why they stay in simpleton paradigms and do some questionable stuff---->  dopamine destructions from melanin loss due to rises in signlet oxygen over triplet state oxygen.

4. The Collapse of the Dielectric Cushion in nnEMF abuse or a magnetic decline can stimulate increases in entropy in the neuroectoderm. 

Forcing the body to digest and circulate amino acids for over 12 hours causes a chronic fluid shift. Because protein synthesis requires high-energy peptide bond formations without the concurrent generation of CCO metabolic water, the Exclusion Zone (EZ) in the cell collapses. The dielectric constant of the intracellular water table sags from its coherent quantum state (~160) back to the bulk water value (~78).

This is why one needs to pay attention to the full effects.  The facade can look good while you sacrifice the brain's cognition = looksmaxxing vs brain cognition

5. Breaking the Isotopic Exhaust Pipe

Deuterium is no longer effectively shoved out into the bulk waste stream. It leaks directly into the Inner Mitochondrial Junctions (IMJs), invading the ATPase nano-motors.

As the K+-stabilized EZ collapses under the weight of the 12-hour protein flood, the cell loses its ability to fractionate deuterium (D+)

6. The Kinetic Isotope Effect (KIE) Stall

The study notes that the molecular signaling markers (mTOR and p70S6K) returned to baseline within 4 hours, but protein synthesis kept going. The biochemists view this "dissociation" as a benefit of sustained substrate availability.

Biophysically, this shows that the cell has lost control of the phase trajectory. The accumulating deuterium creates a massive KIE stall at Complex I. The system is forced into a state of pseudohypoxia and this destroys NAD+ turining it into NADD+. Because the water lattice is deuterated, the cell cannot maintain the CISS effect (Chiral Induced Spin Selectivity).

When CISS is lost triplet oxygen becomes singlet and singlet oxygen is what causes hair loss, weaker collagen, and neural loss.  This is how a 22 yr old body builder dies of cardiac diseases within the SAA.

7.  The Singlet Oxygen (1^O2) Trap

This singlet oxygen acts as a thermal "hot potato," vibrating violently and dumping entropy as heat. This heat denatures neighboring proteins, which explains why the study noted that muscle autophagy markers and protein breakdown remained stubbornly elevated, the cell is actively trying to patch a proteome lattice that is being systematically incinerated by its own internal thermal noise.

Without spin-selectivity, oxygen is no longer reduced cleanly to water at CCO. Instead, it gets trapped in the highly destructive Singlet State (1^O2).  Singlet state destroys tissue coherence and fuels entropy in heat loss that fries hair follicles and brain cells.  It will also yellow your teeth.  

I was thrown off the main stage in 2014 in Pasadena in the Bulletproof conference when I brought this topic up publicly to mitohackers.  Then I was asked to leave the convention center by the organizer when he realized what the implications were of the talk.

8.  The Rockefeller Blind Spot: Labeled Tracers vs. Quantum Automata

The researchers used "intrinsically labeled milk protein" to track amino acid incorporation into the muscle. This is the ultimate "billiard ball" biochemistry mistake:

They track the nitrogen and the carbon, but they completely ignore the magnetic moment of the unpaired electrons and the isotopic mass of the hydrogen attached to those amino acids.

If the milk protein is derived from modern industrial farming (fake food/bulk chemicals), it carries a random, high-deuterium content.

By forcing 100g of this deuterated substrate into the muscle, you are literally "doping" the human semiconductor with heavy isotopes, widening the band gap, and turning the internal particle accelerators into resistors.

The 40% higher protein synthesis rate observed during hours 4–12 isn't a sign of superior health; it is the sound of a quantum computer running a desperate, high-entropy error-correction loop to rebuild structural collagen and myosin that are being damaged by the low-dielectric, deuterium-rich environment.

The funny thing?  This is what the Blau Lab just found.

quoted.text:
For decades, protein metabolism research suggested that muscle protein synthesis plateaus around 20–25 g protein per meal. That conclusion was based on studies measuring anabolic responses over 4–6 hours following moderate protein doses.

A new controlled trial extended the assessment window to 12 hours and tested doses up to 100 g—revealing that the ceiling effect doesn't exist.

Participants consumed 0 g, 25 g, or 100 g intrinsically labeled milk protein following resistance exercise. Researchers applied continuous isotope tracer infusion and measured muscle protein synthesis across multiple tissue fractions.

Myofibrillar protein synthesis rates increased dose-dependently throughout the entire 12-hour period. The 100 g dose produced 40% higher synthesis rates during hours 4–12 compared to 25 g.

Dietary-protein-derived amino acids continued appearing in circulation for over 12 hours following 100 g protein. By the end of the measurement period, only 53% of ingested amino acids had entered circulation—digestion was still ongoing.

Whole-body protein synthesis increased linearly with protein dose. Amino acid oxidation increased minimally—less than 15% of the increment in protein intake was oxidized. Over 85% was directed toward tissue protein synthesis.

Muscle tissue incorporated 13 g of dietary amino acids over 12 hours following the 100 g dose—13% of total intake. Previous estimates from shorter protocols suggested ~10%.

Exogenous amino acids contributed 27% of myofibrillar protein synthesis following 100 g protein versus only 9% following 25 g. The higher dose didn't just extend the duration of synthesis—it fundamentally shifted the precursor pool composition.

Protein breakdown rates didn't differ between treatments. The anabolic response was driven entirely by increased synthesis, not suppressed breakdown.

Molecular signaling showed a different timeline than metabolic outcomes.

mTOR phosphorylation and p70S6K activation peaked within 4 hours and returned to baseline—regardless of dose. But muscle protein synthesis remained elevated beyond 12 hours when 100 g was consumed.

This dissociation demonstrates that anabolic signaling initiates the response, but sustained amino acid availability—not sustained mTOR activation—drives prolonged synthesis.

Muscle free leucine concentrations remained elevated throughout the 12-hour period following 100 g protein. Other amino acids didn't accumulate in the muscle free pool.

Leucine's selective retention supports its dual role: signaling molecule during initial mTOR activation and metabolic substrate during prolonged synthesis.

Current dietary guidelines recommend distributing protein evenly across meals to maximize the anabolic response at each feeding opportunity. That advice assumes the response is transient and saturable.

But if a large protein dose produces sustained anabolism lasting well beyond 12 hours, frequent feeding becomes less critical for optimizing muscle protein synthesis.

Time-restricted feeding protocols—which compress food intake into narrow windows—may not compromise anabolism if sufficient protein is consumed during the feeding period.

The postprandial state may extend far beyond the feeding window when large protein doses are consumed.

The study also challenges assumptions about mTOR, autophagy, and protein turnover.

Ingesting 100 g protein sustained anabolism without suppressing whole-body protein breakdown or muscle autophagy markers. This suggests prolonged anabolism doesn't require a subsequent fasted state to allow protein clearance.

Limitations: The 12-hour measurement window wasn't sufficient to capture the full response to 100 g protein. Digestion and absorption were ongoing when the protocol ended. The magnitude and duration of the response are likely underestimated.

The study was conducted in young men following resistance exercise. Anabolic sensitivity to feeding is lower in sedentary and older populations, so ceiling effects may exist in those groups despite not appearing here.

But the mechanistic data provide clarity on postprandial protein handling at a systems level.

Protein digestion is prolonged. Amino acid absorption is sustained. Exogenous amino acid flux into circulation continues for >12 hours. Tissue uptake doesn't plateau. Incorporation into muscle and plasma protein increases proportionally to dose.

The anabolic response to protein ingestion has no upper limit within the range tested (0–100 g).

Meal size and meal frequency may be more flexible variables than current guidelines suggest. Larger, less frequent meals produce metabolic outcomes comparable to—or exceeding—smaller, more frequent feeding.

posts.thread_title: WHY YOU MUST BE DISCERNING WITH AI
posts.author: Johan Lindøm
posts.content: If you had asked an AI before you hit ENTER on your first two attempts, it would have told you that you sounded like a brick. AI can help us with so many things — diplomacy, dialogue, and much more. The real problem is that there’s a serious need for better communication skills, and I know this is true for me. I’m dyslexic, and these tools have literally saved me by letting me express myself properly. Spellcheck in the ’80s was a game-changer. My kids use tools like Lindy’s, and it’s amazing how much it helps them. I often come across as a brick even though I don’t mean to — I simply never had proper spelling or writing training. Writing everything the wrong way didn’t improve anything. Getting the message written is already hard enough. By the time I try to phrase things in a decent, polite, and thoughtful way, I’m completely out of energy. I learned to be polite at home, but combining that with fighting the words and sentences at the same time is really tough. I’m often perceived as a “brick” or an insensitive person. That’s why getting corrections is so useful. I’ve noticed my skills are improving even without AI now. It’s a real game-changer. Even reading blogs on paper has improved. These improvements also come from the techniques and hacks I’m learning from Doctor Jack. My brain works better.
